metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: EFP Literary Studies and Writing 11 combines in-depth analysis of First Peoples literature with development of students' own writing practice grounded in Indigenous perspectives and literary traditions. Students engage with Indigenous authors and texts while developing voice, crafting narratives, and exploring how literature models possibilities for their own creative and analytical expression. This integrated course emphasizes how reading Indigenous literature informs and strengthens individual writing, and how students can contribute their own voices to Indigenous literary traditions. Through reading, analysis, and writing, students develop critical literacy and creative expression.
metadata.general_description: An integrated exploration of Indigenous literature and writing practice, examining how literary study informs and strengthens student writing.

big_ideas.statement: Reading Indigenous literature provides models for authentic voice, distinctive narrative choices, and how writing can express worldviews and contribute to community.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through studying Indigenous authors' craft—their language choices, narrative structures, imagery, and thematic concerns—students develop understanding of possibilities for their own writing. Indigenous literature models how writers can ground work in cultural identity, address community concerns, and use writing for purposes beyond individual expression.

big_ideas.statement: Sophisticated literary analysis of Indigenous texts develops critical reading skills that transfer to stronger writing and deeper self-awareness as writers.
big_ideas.elaboration: Close attention to how Indigenous authors craft meaning—through structure, characterization, imagery, and language—trains writers to be more intentional in their own choices. Understanding how published Indigenous writers navigate voice, audience, and purpose informs students' development of their own writerly approaches.

big_ideas.statement: Writing grounded in Indigenous perspectives and traditions is a form of cultural participation, identity affirmation, and contribution to ongoing literary and community discourse.
big_ideas.elaboration: When students write from their own perspectives, draw on cultural knowledge, and engage with themes significant to Indigenous communities, their writing becomes more than academic exercise—it becomes meaningful cultural work. Students recognize themselves as potential voices in Indigenous literary traditions and contributors to cultural continuity and community wellbeing.

big_ideas.statement: Revision and craft development strengthen writing, particularly when informed by study of how published Indigenous authors revise and refine their work.
big_ideas.elaboration: Examining how Indigenous writers develop their voices, revise their work, and make deliberate craft choices encourages students to view revision as essential to meaningful writing. Students develop resilience and persistence in their own writing practice by understanding that accomplished writers engage in extensive revision and refinement.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect
categories.raw: Students analyze Indigenous literature and connect literary reading to their own writing development and identity as writers.

competencies.raw: Conduct sophisticated literary analysis of Indigenous texts, examining how authors craft meaning and express distinctive voices
i_can_statements: I can analyze narrative technique, characterization, dialogue, and structure in Indigenous literature
I can explain how Indigenous writers use language, imagery, and symbolism to convey meaning and worldviews
I can identify distinctive voice and style in Indigenous writing and discuss what makes it effective
I can trace how themes and ideas develop across Indigenous literary works and traditions

competencies.raw: Understand how Indigenous literature addresses themes of identity, resilience, land connection, and community responsibility
i_can_statements: I can identify and analyze major themes in Indigenous literature related to identity and cultural persistence
I can explain how Indigenous writers express relationships with land, place, and natural world
I can discuss how Indigenous literature addresses historical trauma, resilience, and healing
I can analyze how Indigenous writers connect personal experience to community concerns and collective identity

competencies.raw: Connect literary analysis to personal writing development, recognizing how studying Indigenous authors informs understanding of craft and voice
i_can_statements: I can articulate how specific Indigenous authors and texts influence my own writing
I can identify literary techniques from Indigenous authors that I want to develop in my own work
I can reflect on how reading Indigenous literature deepens my understanding of voice, audience, and purpose
I can make conscious decisions about my own writing based on study of Indigenous literary craft

categories.name: Create and Communicate
categories.raw: Students develop sophisticated literary analyses and create original writing grounded in Indigenous perspectives and informed by study of Indigenous literature.

competencies.raw: Produce sophisticated written analyses of Indigenous literature that demonstrate deep understanding of texts and literary techniques
i_can_statements: I can write detailed essays analyzing Indigenous literature with attention to craft, themes, and cultural contexts
I can develop original arguments about Indigenous texts and support them with textual evidence
I can integrate research about Indigenous authors and historical contexts into my literary analysis
I can communicate literary insights clearly and persuasively to academic audiences

competencies.raw: Create original written work—narratives, essays, poetry—that draws on Indigenous literary traditions and expresses authentic voice
i_can_statements: I can develop narratives and stories that reflect Indigenous perspectives and values
I can craft writing with distinctive voice grounded in my identity and cultural understanding
I can write poetry, personal essays, or creative pieces inspired by Indigenous literary traditions
I can make intentional choices about structure, language, and imagery in my writing

competencies.raw: Revise and refine writing through multiple drafts, using feedback and literary models to strengthen craft
i_can_statements: I can develop strong thesis statements and arguments in my writing
I can revise for clarity, coherence, and effectiveness, making substantial changes across multiple drafts
I can incorporate feedback from peers and teachers to improve my writing
I can proofread and edit carefully for grammar, mechanics, and citation conventions

areas.name: Indigenous Authors and Literary Traditions
areas.raw: Study of First Peoples literature across genres and traditions, examining distinctive voices, techniques, and the cultural contexts in which Indigenous literature is created
topics: Indigenous novelists and their major works across different time periods
Indigenous poets and distinctive poetic voices and styles
Memoir, autobiography, and personal narrative by First Peoples writers
Short stories and experimental forms in Indigenous literature
Literary traditions specific to different Indigenous nations and communities
Evolution of Indigenous literature and contemporary trends

areas.name: Literary Craft and Voice Development
areas.raw: Analysis of how published Indigenous writers develop distinctive voice, make craft choices, and employ literary techniques; application to student writing
topics: Narrative structure, point of view, and character development in Indigenous literature
Dialogue, voice, and authenticity in Indigenous writing
Imagery, symbolism, and figurative language in Indigenous texts
Tone, mood, and style in Indigenous writing
Opening and closing techniques used by Indigenous authors
Revision strategies and craft development in published Indigenous literature

areas.name: Writing Grounded in Indigenous Perspectives and Experiences
areas.raw: Development of students' own writing practice that draws on cultural identity, personal experience, and Indigenous worldviews
topics: Personal narrative and storytelling grounded in identity and experience
Writing about land, place, and relationships with natural world
Essays and analytical writing addressing community concerns and social justice
Creative writing and poetry informed by Indigenous traditions
Building authentic voice and avoiding stereotypes or appropriation
Writing as cultural participation and contribution to community

areas.name: Reading-Writing Integration and Literary Responsiveness
areas.raw: Examination of how literary reading informs writing; exploration of forms like literary essays, creative adaptations, and response writing
topics: Writing literary responses and reflections on Indigenous texts
Creating original work inspired by or responding to Indigenous literature
Adapting or reimagining Indigenous literary narratives
Writing literary essays that combine analysis and personal voice
Integrating quotations and evidence from Indigenous literature in writing
Building a writing process that incorporates reading, reflection, and revision
